coredata.dc:title: Construction of crosslinked chitosan/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot nanocomposite for hydroxyapatite biomimetic mineralization
coredata.dc:creator: Mallakpour, Shadpour
Khadem, Elham
coredata.dc:description: Crosslinked Chitosan/nitrogen doped-graphene quantum dot nanocomposites (CCS/NGQD NC) with 10 wt% of glutaraldehyd as crosslinker were fabricated under ultrasonic cavitation. Structure and physicochemical properties of the prepared nanocomposites (NC) were systematically characterized by a number of techniques. The swelling capacity of the crosslinked films in the simulated intestinal (pH 7.4) media was evaluated. However, rate of water ingress of specimens was nearly same, but, the CCS/NGQD NC 8 wt% showed the more water absorption ability than the CCS. Bioactivity of the CCS and CCS/NGQD NCs were evaluated by studying their ability to form of apatite on their surface after soaking in a simulated body fluid (SBF) for 30 days. In images of field emission scan electron microscopy, small island-like agglomerated apatite deposited on the sample surface. Also, the pH fluctuation displayed that the hydroxyapatite was formed on the surface of the samples after one month of immersion in SBF. The finding showed that the CCS/NGQD NCs immersed into SBF had a better apatite-forming bioactivity compared with the CCS, which may be due to the high surface area, adequate function groups and appropriate swelling of the NCs. Such improved behaviors of CCS/NGQD NCs may have potential use in the bone tissue engineering.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Graphene quantum dot
Sonication
SBF
Bioactivity

Center of Excellence in Sensors and Green Chemistry Research

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Research Affairs Division of Isfahan University of Technology (IUT) , Isfahan, I. R. Iran; National Elite Foundation, Tehran , I. R. Iran; Iran Nanotechnology Initiative Council, Tehran , I. R. Iran; Center of Excellence in Sensors and Green Chemistry Research, IUT , Isfahan, I. R. Iran.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of a) NGQD, b) CCS, c) CCS/NGQD NC 2 wt%, d) CCS/NGQD NC 5 wt%, and e) CCS/NGQD NC 8 wt%.
Raman spectra of (a) NGQD, (b) CCS/NGQD NC 5 wt% and (c) CCS/NGQD NC 8 wt%.
XRD patterns of NGQD, CCS and CCS/NGQD NCs.
Thermograms of CCS and CCS/NGQD NCs 2, 5, 8 wt%.
(A) UV–vis optical absorption spectra and (B) variation of (αhν)1/2 with the photon energy (hν).
AFM image of the NGQD surface with the height profile.
FE-SEM images of a) CCS, b) CCS/NGQD NC 2 wt%, c) CCS/NGQD NC 5 wt%, and d) CCS/NGQD NC 8 wt%.
TEM images of CCS/NGQD NC 5 wt% in diverse magnifications, and the particle size histogram.
Swelling ratio of CCS and CCS/NGQD NCs at pH 7.4.

A) The pH values of SBF vs. incubation time B) FE-SEM photographs of the surface of the CCS (a–b), CCS/NGQD NC 5 wt% (c–d) and CCS/NGQD NC 8 wt% (e–f) after 30 days soaking in the SBF C) FT-IR spectra after soaking in the SBF.
Preparation of HA on CCS/NGQD NC.
Thermal stability of the CCS and CCS/NGQD NCs.

head.ce:title: Construction of crosslinked chitosan/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot nanocomposite for hydroxyapatite biomimetic mineralization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Organic Polymer Chemistry Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156-83111, Islamic Republic of Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute for Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156-83111, Islamic Republic of Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Institute for Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Organic Polymer Chemistry Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156-83111, Islamic Republic of Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Crosslinked Chitosan/nitrogen doped-graphene quantum dot nanocomposites (CCS/NGQD NC) with 10 wt% of glutaraldehyd as crosslinker were fabricated under ultrasonic cavitation. Structure and physicochemical properties of the prepared nanocomposites (NC) were systematically characterized by a number of techniques. The swelling capacity of the crosslinked films in the simulated intestinal (pH 7.4) media was evaluated. However, rate of water ingress of specimens was nearly same, but, the CCS/NGQD NC 8 wt% showed the more water absorption ability than the CCS. Bioactivity of the CCS and CCS/NGQD NCs were evaluated by studying their ability to form of apatite on their surface after soaking in a simulated body fluid (SBF) for 30 days. In images of field emission scan electron microscopy, small island-like agglomerated apatite deposited on the sample surface. Also, the pH fluctuation displayed that the hydroxyapatite was formed on the surface of the samples after one month of immersion in SBF. The finding showed that the CCS/NGQD NCs immersed into SBF had a better apatite-forming bioactivity compared with the CCS, which may be due to the high surface area, adequate function groups and appropriate swelling of the NCs. Such improved behaviors of CCS/NGQD NCs may have potential use in the bone tissue engineering.

ce:section.ce:para: Chitosan (CS) is a unique amino-polysaccharide risen from treatment of chitin in an intense alkaline solution. This natural polymer is found in primary components of fungal cell walls, insect exoskeletons, and crustaceans [1,2]. Nowadays, development of natural polymers with good bioactivity such as CS due to cell recognition sites, nontoxicity, biocompatibility, biodegradability, and large active surface area have broadly attracted attentions in biomaterials and tissue engineering fields [3–5]. However, some of the factors such as poor mechanical properties, rapid degradation rates, uncontrolled swelling in water and roughness at surface can limit their application. In this regard, cross linking CS (CCS) either by covalent or ionic cross-linking as well as incorporation of nano-fillers like graphene quantum dot (GQD) particles into polymer can have effective role in decrease of these restrictions [6,7].

Glutaraldehyde (GA) is a cross-linking agent, which can react with the amino group of CS and form stable three-dimensional structures with improved mechanical properties and controlled swelling degree [8–10]. Among carbonaceous materials, GQD is a zero dimensional nanomaterial with diameter below 20 nm, which derived from both carbon dots and graphene. The unique properties, like high hydrophilicity, tunable fluorescence, remarkable photostability, low toxicity, high solubility in many solvents, brilliant bioactivity, and biocompatibility make GQD a favorable material in biomedical applications such as bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery, and antimicrobial materials [11–13]. Also, they are used in electronic, electrochemical, and optical applications [14]. It can be due to quantum confinement and edge effects [15]. Doping GQD with nitrogen atom (NGQD) changes the electrical properties and provides more active sites as well as unexpected behavior in material [16]. The presence of functionalized groups such as amine, hydroxyl, carboxyl and epoxy groups on the NGQD surface play an effective role in increment of hydrophilia and biocompatibility [17]. On the other hand, these functional groups by dual interactions such as electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions facilitate linkage of NGQD with macromolecules without the complicated process of covalent bond [18].

The cytotoxicity of different GQDs with diverse cells has been widely assayed by both theoretical and experimental studies over the past few years [19,20]. The results suggested that GQD with small size is really a compound with low cytotoxicity and can be suitable for biomedical application [21]. Sun et al. have prepared a medicated bandage using GQDs and shown that the presence GQD can converse H2O2 into OH radical and improve the antibacterial performance of H2O2 [22]. Zhu et al. [23] and Zhang et al. [24] investigated the ability of GQDs for cellular imaging application. According findings, GQDs indicated better performance for intracellular imaging without the requirement for further surface processing or functionalization than the CdSe and other II-VI type quantum dots [24].

In report of Huang et al., effects of molecular weight (Mw) and degree of deacetylation (DD) on cytotoxicity of chitosan was evaluated. They proved that the CS cytotoxicity was weakened by decreasing polymer DD but was less affected by lowering in Mw [25]. Other agents in toxicity of CS can pointe out to particle size, surface area and surface charge [26].

Autografting and allografting were introduced as a traditional ways to treat bone-defect. However, problems including immune rejection, donor scarcity, supply limitation, and pathogen transfer may be provided by these ways. Up to now expanded researches have been done to repair bone [10]. As pointed out, CS due to promising properties is an ideal candidate in bone graft substituent, but, it cannot imitate all the characteristics of natural bone [27]. On the other hand, combination of hydroxyapatite (HA) with CS can be inappropriate, due to inhomogeneous dispersion and weak interaction between polymer and ceramic [28–30]. It was led to develop biomimetic process and form bone-apatite like carbonate hydroxyapatite layer on polymer in simulated body fluid (SBF). The graphene-based materials are one of the nanomaterials that used as reinforcement to enhance mechanical properties of scaffolds as well as an implant material or a neural chip in the nervous system for tissue engineering [13]. In this regard, the role of these materials in improving mineralization of HA was investigated [31]. For instance, mineralization of CS/graphene oxide (GO) in SBF was studied by Depan and his coworkers [32]. Based on the results of mineralization, the CS/GO composites showed better osteoblasts compatibility compared to the GO and the HA/GO systems. In other work, effect of graphene oxide quantum dots on mineralization properties of polycaprolactone films was studied by Hassanzadeh et al. [33]. Also, from vertically aligned multi-walled carbon nanotube (VAMWCNT) scaffold was used as an inducing template for nano-hydroxyapatite electrodeposition by soaking in SBF [34]. In mineralization process, functional groups (amine, hydroxyl, and carboxyl) of the composite components produce apatite nucleation on their surface in an acellular and protein-free SBF with ion concentrations approximately like to blood plasma [35]. CS due to possesses biocompatibility and antimicrobial activity can be substituted missing or damaged tissue. Production of biological activity HA layer on substance surface is one of the key characteristics of a bioactive material. HA by promoting the adhesion between the implanted materials and host tissues and avoiding the creation of a fibrous capsule may be led to fixation implanted materials in living bone. This substance can be formed from precipitation and evolution of calcium phosphate with time. Predict in-vitro bioactivity (capacity to form a bone tissue) of materials is performed in a SBF solution [36]. Martel-Estrada and et al. [37] investigated in-vitro bioactivity of CS/Mimosa tenuiflora to produce apatite layers on its surface using SBF solution as an incubation medium. The results related to formation of HA were studied by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum and scanning electron microscopy-energy dispersive X-ray analysis.

To the best of our knowledge, no studies have been reported on the bioactivity and apatite-forming ability on the CCS/NGQD NC through biomimetic process. This study has designed a new biocomposite, CCS/NGQD NC, under ultrasonic cavitation as a green, fast and facile method. In this regard, CS was first dissolved in acetic acid and then mixed with varying amounts of NGQD solution before cross-linking with 10 wt% of GA (related to polymer weight). After characterization, swelling property at pH 7.4 and apatite mineralization capability of the CCS/NGQD NCs into SBF at 37 °C for 30 days were examined. The structure and surface of samples were characterized by various techniques.

CS with molecular weight of 600–800 × 103 g·mol−1 was supplied from Acros (New Jersey, USA). Citric acid, urea, glutaraldehyde (GA) 25% and NaOH, NaCl, NaHCO3, KCl, K2HPO4·3H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, Na2SO4, CaCl2, HCl for purveyance of SBF solution were bought from Merck Chemical Co (Germany).

Ultrasonic generation was done by TOPSONICS ultrasonic liquid processor, UP-400 series with power of 100 W and frequency of 25 KHz (Tehran, I. R. Iran). FT-IR was recorded on Jasco-680 FT-IR instrument using KBr pellets in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 and resolution of 4 cm−1. Raman spectroscopy was recorded on a Almega Thermo Nicolet Dispersive Raman Spectrometer using a 532 nm of a Nd:YLF laser from 1300 to 1700 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was measured via Philips X'PERT MPD (Germany) measurements at 2θ angle of 10° to 80° using Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). FE-SEM and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were carried out in HITACHI S-4160 and Philips CM 120, (Netherlands), respectively. Topographic image of samples was investigated using atomic force microscopy (AFM), Digital Multimode Instruments Nanoscope III (Digital Instrument Inc., USA) with noncontact tapping mode with a silica probe (NSC 11) and a frequency of 463 kHz. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer was performed by Jasco-V-570, (Tokyo). Termogravimetric analysis (TGA) instrument (STA503 TA) was applied to study thermal stability of samples from 25 to 800 °C under argon flow and heating rate of 20 °C·min−1.

NGQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid as carbon source in the presence of urea as nitrogen source, adapting the procedure described by Achadu and Nyokong [38] similar to that described by Qu and co-workers [39]. Citric acid (1 mol) and urea (3 mol) were dissolved in 5 mL of water and after obtaining a clear solution, was transferred into 20 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The final product of NGQD was collected by adding ethanol into the dark syrup product. The products was centrifuged and dried at room temperature.

The CCS/NGQD NCs were prepared using a solution casting method under sonochemistry. As a first step, 0.1 g of CS powder was dissolved in 1% acetic acid (8 mL) and ultrasonicated for 5 min. Then, different percentages of NGQD (2, 5, 8 wt%) were separately dissolved in distillated water and added dropwise into the CS solution and intensely stirred for 5 h. After 30 min of ultrasonication, 10 wt% of GA (related to polymer weight) was added to the above mixture under stirring and continuing for 24 h. Then, composites were placed on Petri dishes and after drying, they were immersed in a 2 wt% aqueous NaOH solution and kept for 2 h in order to naturalize the CCS/NGQD NCs. Due to NGQD and GA, the color of CCS/NGQD NCs changed to dark brown.

The swelling behavior of CCS and CCS/NGQD NCs 5 and 8 wt% was investigated by incubating the specimens into buffer solution of simulated intestinal fluid (SIF) containing 20.4 g K2HPO4, 4.8 g NaOH and 3000 mL deionized water (pH = 7.4) [40]. For this purpose, the specimens with an initial weight (Wi) were immersed into the buffer solution and stirred at 37 ± 1 °C at 50 rpm to reach swelling equilibrium. Over prearranged time interval, the samples were isolated from solution, blotted off with tissue paper and re-weighted again (Wf). The swelling ratio (SR) was calculated as follows:
(1)
SR
g / g
=
W f
−
W i
/
W i

In the present study, the standard method defined by Kokubo and Takadama was adopted to test in-vitro the reactivity of bioactive materials [41]. In this test, the bioactivity behavior of the samples was surveyed by exposing the materials in SBF solution without the tris-(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (TRIS) buffer. 100 mg of the each sample was soaked in a polyethylene test bottle containing 10 mL of buffered SBF and kept at 37 °C. After 30 days, the solid samples were separated from the solution by filtration and after three times washing with water and drying, they used for the future characterization.

The scientists deduced that the doped GQDs such as NGQD can show similar behavior as bulk graphene. Formation mechanism of NGQD from citric acid (as carbon source) and urea (as N source) was explained in literatures [39,42]. The prepared NGQD in this work was completely distinguished with different procedures such as FT-IR, FE-SEM, TEM, XRD, energy dispersive X-ray analysis, UV–Vis, TGA, and etc. [38,39]. More functional groups of NGQD, such as NH, OH, COOH, and COOR, placed at the edge of each NGQD behave as a reactive layer at the surface. Thus, this compound can interact with the functional groups of CS through hydrogen bonding and van der walls interactions. When the reaction was performed under ultrasonic irradiation not only increases probability of interactions between constituent materials of the CCS/NGQD NC, but also forms uniform composite in short reaction time. The likely description for the positive usage of sonication is that it could provide the number of active cavitation bubbles. Collapsing of bubbles in vicinity the surface of samples generates high temperature and pressure. This condition is suitable for distribution NGQD into the matrix and preparation of the CCS/NGQD NC (Scheme 1 ). During reaction, a low concentration of GA was used for crosslinking of CS chairs. Reaction between CS and aldehyde was done by Schiff base, which organized by the condensation of an active carbonyl and a primary amine and forming an imine group [43]. Low amounts of GA not only improve physicochemical properties of CS, but also don't block active sites of CS for applications such as adsorption and mineralization tests.

In FT-IR spectrum of the NGQD (Fig. 1 a), the broad absorption band at 3100–3500 cm−1 was attributed to the NH/OH stretching vibration. The asymmetric and symmetric CH bond stretching vibrations were appeared at 2930 and 2857 cm−1. The peaks occurring around 1572 and 1384 cm−1 are assigned to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of the COO– present on the edges of the NGQDs [44]. The characteristic stretching vibrations at around 1417 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 can be attributed to the CN bond and CO functional group [45]. Also, no characteristic absorption bands of CH in aromatic rings (3000–3100 cm−1) and CC of aromatic rings (1450–1650 cm−1) are included in this FT-IR spectrum, implying no any aromatic ring is comprised in the NGQD.

In the spectrum of CS, the peaks at 3420 cm−1, and 2860–2919 cm−1 are correspond to OH and NH2 stretching vibrations, and CH stretching vibration, respectively. The presence of 1653 cm−1 band assigns to CO stretching of amide I, the 1598 cm−1 band to NH bending, the 1421 cm−1 band to CH2 bending, and band in 1383 cm−1 is corresponded to CH symmetrical bending vibration [46]. Depending on the reaction condition, CS can undergo different reactions with GA, that herein the amino groups of CS involve cross-linking reaction with aldehyde terminals of the GA to form an imine group. In FT-IR spectrum of crosslinked CS (Fig. 1b), a peak at 1640 cm−1 belongs to the valence vibrations of the CN bond, which coincided with amide I. In other hand, there is no signature of the band of free aldehyde group at 1720 cm−1, which can be attributed to low concentration of GA in the CCS. In the spectra of CCS/NGQD NCs with different percentages, all the characteristic peaks of CCS and NGQD were appeared with slight difference in the peaks intensity. Meanwhile, the absorbance band corresponding to OH group at 3434 cm−1 and the residual NH2 from crosslinking at 1598 cm−1 red shifted toward a lower frequencies (1596 and 3428 cm−1, respectively). These changes may be owning to the synergistic effect of H-bonding associations between the functional groups of NGQD and CCS.

Raman spectroscopy was applied to examine of interaction between NGQD and CCS in polymeric NCs. Raman spectra of NGQD, CCS/NGQD NCs 5 and 8 wt% (Fig. 2 ) depicted the two broad peaks at 1381 cm−1 and 1589 cm−1, corresponding to characteristic D and G bands of NGQD (Fig. 2). The D band is assigned to a breathing mode of A1g symmetry from sp3 defects that breakdown the selection rule and symmetry, and the crystalline G band attributed to an E2g mode from in plane vibration of sp2 bonded carbons. After loading NGQD into the polymer matrix, the D-band shifted to lower frequency (red shift) (1370 cm−1), which is more prominent [47]. The D to G peak intensities (I
D /I
G ) ratio of these characteristic bands has been used to evaluate the structural disorders of the samples [48]. The I
D /I
G ratios for NGQD, CCS/NGQD NCs 5 and 8 wt% were found to be 0.61, 0.94, and 0.74, respectively. Increasing values of I
D /I
G ratios in NCs demonstrated decrease of the sp2 domain and increase of the sp3 defects during hydrogen bond interactions of NGQD with CCS. This increase also revealed a reduction in the content of oxide functional groups, which may be owning to the nucleophilic substitution between the active sites of CS and NGQD. The extra peaks in Fig. 2b and c may be due to presence of CS [49].

The XRD pattern of NGQD shows a wide peak around 2θ = 22° with (002) crystal plane of hexagonal graphite (Fig. 3 ). This wide peak in the NGQD can be related to the reduced oxygen groups as a result of the nitrogen doping or surface defects on the NGQD structure. It should be noted that no peak of CA is observed in the XRD pattern, which implied to carbonize CA to NGQD. The crystalline structure of the CCS and CCS/NGQD NCs are shown in Fig. 3. The consistent with the findings of a number of previous reports, the crystallinity of the CCS reduces than the pure CS at 19.9° [50]. This could be attributed to the deformation of the strong inter- and intra-molecular hydrogen bonds in the original CS, which efficiently destroyed the regularity of the CS chains due to cross-linking with GA. However, decrease of crystallinity in CCS is low that it is due to low concentration of the used GA. After the addition of NGQD, the XRD of CCS/NGQD NCs have similar diffraction peaks with the CCS with slight difference in width of characteristic peak that shifted to higher angles (2θ = 20.7°). This difference in XRD pattern of CCS/NGQD NCs can be assigned to the trace amount of NGQDs and hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions between NGQD and polymer chains.

UV–vis adsorption spectra of CCS and CCS/NGQD NC are displayed in Fig. 5A. Same to the UV–vis results reported by Qu and et al. [39], the NGQD showed two absorption bands at 340 and 239 nm (inset of Fig. 5A). These absorption peaks are respectively corresponded to n → π* transition of the CO and CN bond and the π → π* electronic transition of aromatic sp2 domains (CC) in sp3 C matrix [39,51,52]. Notably, no peaks of urea and CA were observed in the UV–vis spectrum, which implying purity of the specimen. The UV–vis pattern of CCS revealed fundamental adsorption in the wavelengths shorter, approximately 250 nm [53]. UV–vis spectra of the CCS/NGQD NCs confirmed the presence of the NGQD in the CCS matrix. Also, the intensity of these bands increases considerably with respect to increase of NGQD amount inside the polymer matrix. This probably is relative to the positive interaction and electron transition between the functional groups of GQDs and the heteroatoms of the polymer [54,55]. According to results have been reported by Song and et al. [56] energy gap of graphenes increased as the size reduced. In previous works, the amount of energy gap (Eg) was calculated by photoluminescence. While, in this work from tauc's argument (Eq. (2)) was used for this aim;
(2)
αhν =
α 0
hν −
E g
n
where constants of α0 and h are called the band tailing and Planck's constant, respectively. ν, and n are the frequency, and constant related to the different modes of transitions. Intercept of the extrapolation to x-axis directly affords energy gap. According to equation and Fig. 5B, values of energy gap in the maximum absorbance peak of NGQD and CCS/NGQD NC 8 wt% were estimated to be about 4.0 and 3.9 eV, respectively.

TEM, FE-SEM, and AFM images of NGQD already have been characterized [38,39], and in this work, we only used the AFM images to more characterize it. Based on AFM measurement (Fig. 6 ), nano-sheets size of GQD was mostly 10 nm, whose height varies from 5 nm to 15 nm with a uniform distribution. Various sizes of NGQD were observed in AFM image, which it can be due to lack of using a dialysis membrane.

FE-SEM micrographs of the CCS and CSS/NGQD NCs with various mass ratios of NGQD are indicated in Fig. 7 . CCS in the absent of NGQD displays a rubble-like structure with the homogenous and compact surface. It is noticeable that the surface morphologies of the CCS/NGQD NCs are dissimilar from CCS before loading. When, NGQD was ultrasonicated and incubated in the polymer solution, polymer chains wrapped and adsorbed at the surface of NGQD. In Fig. 7b–d, surface of CCS/NGQD NCs shown an uneven structure with slight cracks and ruptures on the surface, which provided the effective area for pollution adsorption.

The TEM images of CCS/NGQD NC 5 wt% with the different scales and histogram of the particles size distribution were indicated in Fig. 8 . Although, it is intricate that provided high quality of TEM micrographs owning to low electron density between the quantum dots and carbon coated TEM grids. Nevertheless, the acquired images revealed nearly homogeneous distribution of NGQD (dark spot) into the CCS matrix (light area), which prepared under sonication method. Base on histogram of the TEM (obtained by software of Digimizer and SPSS statistics data), the average size of NGQDs loaded into the polymeric NC was estimated about 4.2 nm. Compared with the NGQD size obtained with the AFM analysis, the CCS/NGQD NC 5 wt% shows an approximate decrease in diameter of the quantum dots, which affords of the irradiation of ultrasonic during the formation of the polymeric NC 5 wt%.

The swelling ratio of specimens is intensely affected by their chemical composition. The swelling properties of CCS and CCS/NGQD NC 5 wt% are presented in Fig. 9 . The equilibrium swelling of the specimens was achieved after 3 h incubation in buffer solution, and afterward no significant change was observed. Also, during swelling process no notable disintegration was noticed and films were well maintained even after 10 h of soaking into buffer solution. Results revealed that rate of water ingress of specimens was approximately same while, swelling degree was low due to crosslinking of polymer. Of all specimens, CCS showed the lowest water absorption (2.31 ± 0.41). While, this characteristic was enhanced to 2.63 ± 0.31 in CCS/NGQD NC 5 wt% and 3.07 + 0.31 g/g in CCS/NGQD NC 8 wt%. It is due to the hydrophilic nature of NGQD into the polymer matrix that increased the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in samples and by dispersion into the polymer decreased polymer-polymer interaction between CCS chairs. On the other hand, at pH 7.4 (alkali medium) the COOH and OH groups on the compounds became charged ions, inducing to swelling due to the enhancement of electrostatic repulsion between these ionized active groups and formation of osmotic pressure [57]. This result is in agreement with previously reported data [58,59]. Correia et al. [60] investigated swelling properties of CS in the presence of bioactive glass (BG). The results showed that the uptake capability of water/ethanol (with various volume percentages) in CS/BG NCs was higher than the pure CS, due to the hydrophilic nature of the BG fraction. For instance, the swelling percentage for the pure CS in 100 vol% of water reached 179 vol% and for CS/BG NCs reached 358 vol%. In other work, the presence of zinc oxide (ZnO) NPs into CS hydrogels was caused to enhance swelling properties in compared to the pure CS hydrogel [61]. This result was related to form CS/ZnO networks with pores and free space as well as naturalize the osmotic pressure. While in other study, Mesquita et al. [62] showed hydrophilicity and swelling properties of CS NCs remarkably decreased with increasing amount of cellulose nanocrystals (CNC). For example, at 10 wt% of CNC, the water uptake decreased to approximately 25% of the value observed for pure CS. It may be due to high degree of crystallinity and tortuous path that created in high concentrations of CNC.

pH changes of solid samples during the soaking in SBF solution were shown in Fig. 10A. In SBF solution, due to lack TRIS buffer was observed oscillations in pH value [63]. During the first 5 days, pH value reaches its maximum and after that pH undergoes a decrease. All samples presented successive pH changes at immersion times, which finally reached a nearly constant amount. The fastest increment is identified for the CCS/NGQD NC 5 wt% and the slowest one for the CCS/NGQD NC 8 wt%, while the CCS shows intermediate behavior. In bioactivity process the presence of functional groups such as COOH and OH and also NH2 is very important to nucleate and produce HA [64,65]. In tissue engineering field, suitable mechanical properties, chemical stability, and low degradation rate by enzymes are important factors [66,67]. There is no hydrogel that complies all of the demands of an ideal scaffold. Accordingly, various approaches like cross linking was proposed for creating a stable, three-dimensional (3D), and tunable structure in aqueous environments [68,69]. In this work, to better performance of CCS/NGQD NCs and presence sufficient functional groups, optimum amount of GA (10 wt%) was used as cross-linker. During bioactive process, the functional group such as COOH is deprotonated and negatively charged at pH 7.4. Therefore, the active sites as Lewis bases selectively absorb the positively charged calcium ions in SBF. As the calcium ions collected, the surface became positively charged and linked with the negatively charged phosphate ions, forming an amorphous calcium phosphate through the intake of PO4 3−, Ca2+, OH– and CO3 2– from SBF solution. This phase was metastable and eventually transformed into the stable crystalline bone-like apatite (Scheme 2 ), [31,70]. Therefore, a decrease in the pH value can be associated to the ionization of carboxyl groups, while increment in the pH value related to consumption of phosphate and carbonate ions during extended nucleation and formation of apatite (Fig. 10A). FE-SEM micrographs of CCS and CCS/NGQD NCs obtained after soaking in SBF for one month are shown in Fig. 10B. As seen, the surface roughness of samples was increased and small island-like agglomerated apatite deposited on the surface after incubation in the SBF solution. These changes observed specific in the CCS/NGQD NC 5 wt% can be associated to presence of NGQD containing of carboxyl groups which stimulates the nucleation and crystallization of HA into the CCS NC [71].

In the FT-IR spectra of the mineralized samples (Fig. 10C), peaks revealed at 565–610 cm−1 and 1021 cm−1 are assigned to phosphate and 1456 cm−1 associated to carbonate group. All spectra of CCS and CCS/NGQD NCs were analogous, without progression observable in either peak intensities or locations and only the CCS/NGQD NC 5 wt% is displayed as typical. Reason of this phenomenon maybe owning to surface-dominated of spectra and somewhat small amounts of NGQD into CCS. It has good agreement with data, which described previously by other researchers. For example, Liu and et al. [72] functionalized surface of graphene oxide by reduction and oxidation polymerization of dopamine. After incubating in a SBF, dopamine layers provide an efficient interaction with mineral ions and improve the bioactivity and formation of the HA nanoparticles. Mallakpour and Khadem [73] prepared CS/calcium carbonate-γ-aminopropyl triethoxy silane (CS/CC-ATS) NCs under ultrasonic irradiation and found that the CS with 5 wt% of CC-ATS has more bioactivity ability than the pure CS. This nucleation and crystallization of HA on the NC surface may be related to the presence of CC-ATS NPs into polymer. Yang and et al. [31] investigated effect of low concentration of multiwall carbon nanotube (MWNT) on crystal size of HA. The found that the presence MWNT with COOH groups facilitated nucleation of apatite and decreased average size it from 22 to 10–14 nm. Ðošic his co-workers [74] prepared ternary composite based on HA/chitosan/graphene on Ti substrates by cathodic electrophoretic deposition. The results showed that the bioactivity of HA/CS/graphene composites is better than the HA/CS. It may be attributed to presence of graphene nano-sheets that provide walls or the cross-sections to more nucleation of HA crystals. However, this ternary composite displays the maximum corrosion resistance and the minimum corrosion rate in SBF, owning to a thick HA layer on its surface.

This work offers the crosslinked chitosan NCs incorporated with NGQD as filler and investigates their structure characteristics and bioactivity potential in SBF in-vitro. During process, NGQD was successfully prepared using hydrothermal rout and after dispersing into CS matrix under ultrasonic irradiation, cross-linked with GA by Schiff base. The chemical structure of CCS/NGQD NCs was confirmed by FT-IR, Raman spectroscopy and XRD. Thermal stability of CCS matrix was increased by successful insertion and dispersion of NGQD into the polymer. The swelling properties of CCS/GQD NCs in buffer solution (pH 7.4) were more than the pure CCS. This result showed that presence of the hydrophilic NGQD increased the hydrophilicity of polymeric NCs that is suitable for bioactivity. An in-vitro bioactive assay was performed in SBF to study samples ability to form apatite crystalline phase. The performed studies by FE-SEM images, FT-IR and pH measurements confirmed that a HA layer was formed on the surface of the samples after immersion in SBF for 30 days. Of all samples, CCS/NGQD NC 5 wt% showed most bioactivity behavior than the other samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Research Affairs Division of Isfahan University of Technology (IUT), Isfahan, I. R. Iran; National Elite Foundation, Tehran, I. R. Iran; Iran Nanotechnology Initiative Council, Tehran, I. R. Iran; Center of Excellence in Sensors and Green Chemistry Research, IUT, Isfahan, I. R. Iran.
